text: The laws of HCI design are not as strict as other kinds of laws. They're really fuzzy actually. The two most famous are Fitt's law & Hick's law. Both of them are based on the theory of information. That's a quite solid foundation and the laws "make sense". Recently I've learned about a new one - "SNARC effect". Apparently it was discovered in the 90s and is still under active research. Google it - it's quite an interesting one! And nobody quite knows why it occurs. There is no underlying theory. For some individuals it may appear, disappear or even reverse - but for groups it's reliably present. It happens in people, chickens and even bees - but for some reason not so much in professional mathematicians. Interestingly (but not surprisingly) it also goes against some of the established programming terminology like "shift left" / "shift right" to multiply / divide number by 2. What really makes this interesting for me is the source of the effect. That's still a mystery but I think that once it's nailed down, it'll lead to a bunch of other, related effects, that could be exploited in UI design!

text: First time hearing about SNARC! fascinating. We count on our hands left to right too.
